village_facts: Bhairkhanpalle (also spelled Bhairkhanpally/Bairkhanpally) is a village and gram panchayat in Keshampet (Keshampeta) mandal, Telangana, India; it was in Mahbubnagar district until the October 2016 districts reorganisation, when it moved to Ranga Reddy district (sources: villageinfo.in, geolysis.com, onefivenine.com, cckraopedia blog).
PIN code is 509408, served by Keshampet Sub Office (sources: getpincodes.com, pincodearea.in, thanksmygov.com, villageinfo.in).
The village lies about 5 km from the mandal/sub-district headquarters Keshampet and about 80 km from the former district headquarters Mahbubnagar (sources: onefivenine.com, villageinfo.in).

Census 2011: total population 1,066 (541 male, 525 female) in 248 households; sex ratio ~970; children age 0-6: 166; Scheduled Castes 232 (~21.8%) and Scheduled Tribes 183 (~17.2%) (sources: census2011.co.in, villageinfo.in, geolysis.com).
Total geographical area is 714 hectares, with a population density of about 149 persons per sq km (sources: villageinfo.in, geolysis.com).
Literacy rate is low: 43.43% overall (males ~54.5%, females ~32%) per Census 2011 (sources: villageinfo.in, onefivenine search snippet).

The village has a government primary school and a government middle school; higher education and most health facilities (PHC etc.) are 5-10+ km away; a bank is available in the village and a post office within 2-5 km (sources: villageinfo.in, geolysis.com).
The village is connected by public bus service and all-weather roads, but has no railway station within 10 km (sources: villageinfo.in, geolysis.com).

Keshampet mandal (of which Bhairkhanpalle is one of 20 revenue villages) had a 2011 population of 42,453 across 21,514 hectares; main crops are maize and cotton (source: cckraopedia.blogspot.com, te.wikipedia.org Keshampet mandal page).

Keshampet mandal/Bhairkhanpalle area is bordered by Farooqnagar and Balanagar mandals to the west, Talakondapalle mandal to the east, and Kothur mandal to the north (source: onefivenine.com via search snippet).
how_to_reach: From Hyderabad: roughly 64 km to Keshampet bus station, about 1 hour 23 minutes by road (source: alldistancebetween.com); the usual route is NH44 (Hyderabad-Bangalore highway) south past Kothur to Shadnagar, then the Shadnagar-Keshampet road; the village is ~5 km beyond Keshampet.
NH44 (old NH7) is about 12.7 km from Keshampet via Shadnagar Road, so the village is reached by leaving NH44 near Shadnagar (source: onefivenine.com Keshampet town page snippet).
Hyderabad to Shadnagar is approximately 50-60 km via NH44 or the Outer Ring Road, taking about 1-1.5 hours (source: openplots.net).

Shadnagar to Keshampet is listed as 24 km by road (~32 minutes) on distancesfrom.com; sources vary and the actual turn-off distance from NH44 may be shorter (~13 km per onefivenine), so treat distances as approximate.

Nearest railway stations: Shadnagar (SHNR) about 21 km from Keshampet, and Jadcherla about 34 km; there is no railway station within 10 km of Keshampet or the village (sources: onefivenine.com snippet, villageinfo.in).

Timmapur (TMX) railway station, located on Chegur Road near Kothur (PIN 509325, Rangareddy district), is another nearby SCR station about 15 km from Shadnagar; MMTS/passenger trains from Hyderabad (Kacheguda side) stop at both Timmapur and Shadnagar (sources: mappls.com, indiarailinfo.com, trainspy.com).

By bus: the village itself is connected by public bus service (TGSRTC), and Keshampet has its own bus station reachable from Hyderabad (MGBS side); from Keshampet, local buses/autos cover the last ~5 km (sources: villageinfo.in, alldistancebetween.com, tgsrtc.telangana.gov.in).
nearby: Keshampet - mandal headquarters town, ~5 km away (villageinfo.in, onefivenine.com)
Shadnagar - nearest significant town and rail/road hub on NH44, ~21-24 km (distancesfrom.com, onefivenine snippet)
Kothur - town/mandal to the north on NH44, with Timmapur railway station nearby (onefivenine snippet, mappls.com)
Farooqnagar, Balanagar and Talakondapalle - adjacent mandals (onefivenine snippet)

Rameshwaram (Ramalingeswara Swamy) Temple at Farooqnagar - a locally famous Shiva temple about 20 km from Shadnagar near the old NH7; legend attributes the linga's installation to Lord Rama (omastrology.com, templesinindiainfo.com)
Statue of Equality (Sri Ramanujacharya) / Chinna Jeeyar Swamy Ashram and Divya Saketham at Muchintal - major spiritual landmarks ~8 km from Kothur (myopenplots.com)
Ratnalayam Temple near Kothur/Shadnagar - notable Venkateswara temple attraction in the area (myopenplots.com)

daily_schedule.item: Temple opens – Suprabhata Seva (awakening of the Lord)
Abhishekam to Sri Lakshmi Narasimha Swamy
Sarva Darshanam (general darshan); sevas performed on request
Madhyahna Dwarabandhanam – temple closes for the afternoon
Sayana/Pavalimpu Seva (putting the Lord to rest)

sevas.name: Ashtottara Satanamarchana
sevas.description: Archana chanting the 108 names of Sri Lakshmi Narasimha Swamy in the devotee's name and gotram. Performed daily during darshan hours.

sevas.name: Sahasranamarchana
sevas.description: Archana with the 1,008 names of the Lord, offered with kumkuma and tulasi for the devotee's gotram and family. (Yadadri charges ₹216 for this seva.)

sevas.name: Kshirabhishekam
sevas.description: Sacred bath of the moolavirat with milk, believed to cool and please the Lord's ugra (fierce) aspect. A favourite offering at Narasimha kshetras.

sevas.name: Abhishekam (Nija/Salagrama Abhishekam)
sevas.description: Full abhishekam with panchamritam (milk, curd, ghee, honey, sugar), followed by alankarana and harathi. Performed in the early morning; devotees may participate as sankalpa karta.

sevas.name: Swamy Vari Kalyanotsavam
sevas.description: The celestial wedding of Sri Lakshmi Devi with Narasimha Swamy, performed for the sponsoring couple's wellbeing, marital harmony and progeny. The most auspicious seva at any Lakshmi Narasimha temple.

sevas.name: Sri Satyanarayana Swamy Vratam
sevas.description: Vratam and katha performed at the temple, commonly booked on Ekadashi, Pournami and after weddings or housewarmings.

sevas.name: Sudarshana Narasimha Homam
sevas.description: Fire ritual to Sudarshana and Narasimha for removal of obstacles, doshas and fear; performed on request or on special days.

sevas.name: Annadanam Sponsorship
sevas.description: Sponsor free meals (prasadam) for devotees on a day of your choice — birthdays, anniversaries and remembrance days. 'Annadanam is Maha Danam.'

sevas.name: Nitya Deeparadhana / Shashvata Seva
sevas.description: One-time endowment in the donor's name; archana or deeparadhana is performed every year on a chosen tithi (birthday/gotram day), following the shashvata seva custom of Mattapalli and Yadadri.

sevas.name: Vahana Pooja
sevas.description: Blessing of new vehicles at the temple entrance with archana and coconut offering.

festivals.name: Sri Narasimha Jayanti
festivals.description: The most important festival of the temple, celebrating the Lord's manifestation from the pillar to save Prahlada. Marked by special abhishekam, Swamy Vari Kalyanotsavam in the evening (the Lord appeared at twilight), and annadanam for all devotees.

festivals.name: Annual Brahmotsavam / Jatara
festivals.when: Annually, dates fixed by panchangam (village jataras commonly fall in Phalguna–Chaitra, February–April)
festivals.description: The temple's annual multi-day utsavam with Dhwajarohanam, daily vahana sevas, Swamy Vari Kalyanam, Rathotsavam (chariot procession around the village) and grama bhojanam, on the pattern of the famous Dharmapuri and Yadadri Brahmotsavams.

festivals.name: Sri Rama Navami
festivals.description: Sita Rama Kalyanam is performed in the temple mandapam with panakam and vadapappu distributed as prasadam, as in every Vaishnava temple of Telangana.

festivals.name: Vaikuntha Ekadashi (Mukkoti Ekadashi)
festivals.description: Devotees pass through the specially opened northern entrance (Uttara Dwara Darshanam) for the Lord's darshan, believed to grant the merit of entering Vaikuntham. Day-long bhajans and fasting.

festivals.name: Ugadi
festivals.when: Chaitra Shuddha Padyami (March–April) – Telugu New Year
festivals.description: The new year begins with panchanga sravanam (reading of the almanac) at the temple and distribution of Ugadi pachadi to all devotees.

festivals.name: Hanuman Jayanti (Telugu tradition)
festivals.description: Observed in Telangana on Vaishakha Bahula Dashami, concluding the 41-day Hanuman Deeksha that begins on Chaitra Purnima. Special pooja to Anjaneya Swamy with Sundarakanda parayanam and annadanam.

content.narasimha_story:
The story of Lord Narasimha, the fourth avatara of Sri Maha Vishnu, is told in the seventh skandha of the Srimad Bhagavatam. The demon king Hiranyakashipu, burning with hatred for Vishnu after the Lord's Varaha avatara slew his brother Hiranyaksha, performed severe penance and won an extraordinary boon from Brahma: he could be killed neither by man nor beast, neither by day nor by night, neither inside a dwelling nor outside, neither on the earth nor in the sky, and by no weapon. Armored by this boon, he declared himself god of all the worlds and forbade the very name of Hari in his kingdom.

Yet in his own palace was born the greatest of devotees — his son Prahlada, who from the womb itself was devoted to Sri Hari. Neither the threats of his teachers nor the tortures of his father — poison, fire, trampling by elephants, being hurled from cliffs — could shake the child's faith, for the Lord protected him in every trial. Enraged, Hiranyakashipu finally roared, "If your Hari is everywhere, is he in this pillar?" "He is," replied Prahlada calmly. When the king struck the pillar with his mace, it split open with a thunderous roar, and the Lord emerged in a form never seen before — Narasimha, half man and half lion, honouring every word of Brahma's boon. At twilight (neither day nor night), on the threshold of the court (neither inside nor outside), placing the demon on His lap (neither earth nor sky), the Lord, who was neither man nor beast, tore Hiranyakashipu apart with His nails, which were no weapons.

Even after the demon fell, the Lord's fury blazed on, and none of the devas dared approach Him — until the child Prahlada walked up fearlessly and offered prayers at His feet, and Goddess Lakshmi Devi came and seated Herself beside the Lord. Soothed by Her presence and by Prahlada's devotion, the Ugra (fierce) Narasimha became Shanta (peaceful) — Lakshmi Narasimha, the form in which He is worshipped in this temple. With Amma Lakshmi Devi on His lap, the Lord who destroyed evil for the sake of one devotee's faith sits here as the compassionate protector of all who call on Him, granting courage, health and freedom from fear.

slokas.name: Sri Narasimha Maha-Mantra (Ugram Viram)
slokas.sanskrit:
ॐ उग्रं वीरं महाविष्णुं ज्वलन्तं सर्वतोमुखम् ।
नृसिंहं भीषणं भद्रं मृत्योर्मृत्युं नमाम्यहम् ॥
slokas.translation: Om. I bow to Lord Narasimha, the fierce and heroic Maha Vishnu, blazing with radiance and facing all directions; terrifying to evil yet supremely auspicious, He is the death of death itself. (Chanted for protection and freedom from fear.)

slokas.name: Sri Narasimha Gayatri
slokas.sanskrit:
ॐ वज्रनखाय विद्महे तीक्ष्णदंष्ट्राय धीमहि ।
तन्नो नारसिंहः प्रचोदयात् ॥
slokas.translation: Om. May we know the Lord of adamantine (thunderbolt-like) nails; may we meditate on Him of sharp fangs. May that Lord Narasimha illumine and impel our intellect.

slokas.name: Sri Lakshmi Narasimha Karavalamba Stotram (opening verse, by Adi Shankaracharya)
slokas.sanskrit:
श्रीमत्पयोनिधिनिकेतन चक्रपाणे भोगीन्द्रभोगमणिराजितपुण्यमूर्ते ।
योगीश शाश्वत शरण्य भवाब्धिपोत लक्ष्मीनृसिंह मम देहि करावलम्बम् ॥
slokas.translation: O Lord who dwells in the Ocean of Milk, bearer of the discus, whose holy form is adorned by the gems on the hoods of Adisesha; Lord of yogis, eternal refuge, the boat that carries us across the ocean of worldly existence — O Lakshmi Narasimha, give me the support of Your hand.

sources: https://yadagiriguttatemple.telangana.gov.in/timings/ — Official Yadadri (Yadagirigutta) devasthanam daily seva schedule (Suprabhatam, Nijabhishekam, Archana, Nitya Kalyanotsavam, Aragimpu)

https://templesinindiainfo.com/yadagirigutta-sri-lakshmi-narasimha-swamy-vari-devastanam-seva-pooja-price-details/ — Yadagirigutta seva price list (Nijabhishekam ₹250/₹500, Sahasranamarchana ₹216, Kalyanotsavam ₹1,250, Sudarshana Homam ₹1,116, Ashtottaram ₹100) used as price anchors

https://www.tirthayatra.org/yogananda-narasimha-temple-mattapalli/ — Mattapalli temple daily schedule (5 AM Suprabhatam, midday Dvarabandhanam, 4 PM reopen, 7:30 PM Kavatabandhanam) — model for a smaller temple's hours
https://gotirupati.com/vadapalli-narasimha-swamy-temple-timings-and-darshan/ — Vadapally Lakshmi Narasimha temple timings (6 AM–1 PM, 3 PM–7:30 PM)
http://srinarasimhakutumbam.org/temples/divya-kshetra/mattapalli/ — Mattapalli shashvata seva types (Sasvata Kalyanam, Abhishekam, Sahasranama Archana, Ashtottara Puja)
https://www.drikpanchang.com/dashavatara/narasimha/narasimha-jayanti-date-time.html — Narasimha Jayanti on Vaishakha Shukla Chaturdashi (30 April 2026)

https://www.sakshipost.com/news/telangana/dharmapuri-brahmotsavam-begins-today-march-10-385522 — Dharmapuri Lakshmi Narasimha Swamy 13-day Brahmotsavam/Jatara from Phalguna Shuddha Ekadashi (March), with Kalyanam, Teppotsavam, Rathotsavam
https://www.trawell.in/telangana/karimnagar/dharmapuri — Dharmapuri temple festivals (Brahmotsavam, Narasimha Navaratri, Vaikuntha Ekadashi)
https://greenmesg.org/stotras/narasimha/ugram_viram.php — Devanagari text and translation of the Ugram Viram Narasimha mantra
https://www.iskconbangalore.org/narasimha-gayatri-mantra-meditation/ — Narasimha Gayatri transliteration and meaning cross-check
https://vignanam.org/devanagari/lakshmi-nrusimha-karavalamba-stotram.html — Lakshmi Nrusimha Karavalamba Stotram, Devanagari text (verse 1)
https://te.wikipedia.org/wiki/యాదగిరి_లక్ష్మీనరసింహస్వామి_దేవాలయం — Telugu Wikipedia article verifying Telugu script rendering of the temple name and terms (దేవాలయం, దర్శన వేళలు)
https://www.drikpanchang.com/telugu/festivals/hanuman-jayanthi/hanuman-jayanthi-date-time.html — Telugu Hanuman Jayanthi on Vaishakha Bahula Dashami (12 May 2026)
https://iskcontemple.org/en/blogs/narasimha-chaturdashi-30-april-2026 — Narasimha Chaturdashi story and significance; Srimad Bhagavatam canto 7 narrative cross-check

common_sections: Announcement/flash-news strip (thin ticker above or below header — festival dates, timing changes)
Sticky header: temple name in Telugu + English, small emblem/logo, nav links, prominent Donate button
Hero: full-width gopuram/deity photograph or slider, temple name as display heading, Sanskrit/Telugu invocation line (e.g. a Narasimha sloka), 1-2 CTAs (Donate / Visit)
About / Sthala Puranam (history + mythological significance of the deity and the village kshetram)
Darshan & temple timings (morning/evening blocks, ritual-by-ritual schedule table)
Sevas & Pujas (card grid: seva name, short description, ticket price in ₹, booking/contact note)
Festivals & events (Brahmotsavam, Narasimha Jayanti, Kalyanotsavam — dates or month, upcoming-first)
Photo gallery (deity alankaram, temple exterior, festival photos)
Donations (Annadanam, temple development/Nitya Puja funds — with bank/UPI details and trust info)
How to reach / accommodation (map, directions from nearest town, satram/room info if any)
Contact + footer (phone numbers by purpose, address, EO/committee credit, quick links, social icons)
design_conventions: Palette: deep maroon (#6b1d1d–#8b0000 range) or saffron/kumkum orange as primary, gold/brass (#c9a227–#d4af37) accents, ivory/cream (#fdf8ef) backgrounds — never pure white or pure black; premium sites use ONE dominant dark tone with restrained gold, dated sites splash saffron+red+yellow+green together

Typography: high-contrast serif display face for English headings (Cormorant Garamond, Playfair Display, or Cinzel for a carved-stone feel) paired with a clean sans (Inter, Poppins, Mulish) for body; all-caps letter-spaced kickers ('DARSHAN TIMINGS') read premium

Telugu text: use Google Fonts — Noto Serif Telugu for headings/deity names, Noto Sans Telugu or Mandali for body, Ramabhadra for bold display; declare a font stack like 'Noto Serif Telugu, serif' scoped to Telugu strings so mixed EN/TE lines don't fall back to system fonts

Motifs used well: a single repeated device — thin gold rule with a kalasham or lotus glyph centered as section dividers, subtle mandala/paisley background watermark at 3-5% opacity, gopuram silhouette in footer or hero bottom edge, temple-arch (prabhavali) framing on the deity photo, small diya/lotus icons for list bullets

Premium = restraint: generous whitespace, cream sections alternating with one deep maroon 'inverted' section (donations or timings), consistent card grid with hairline gold borders and soft shadows, one high-quality hero photo with a dark gradient overlay for text legibility, scroll-reveal animations kept subtle

Dated 2010 look to avoid: rainbow gradients, blinking/marquee text, beveled buttons, Comic Sans/Brush Script, clip-art Om symbols, tiled repeating god-image backgrounds, low-res stretched photos, visitor counters, autoplaying bhajan audio, centered walls of text
Iconography: line-style (stroke) icons in gold for timings/phone/location beat colorful emoji or 3D clip art; deity imagery should be a few large high-quality photos rather than many small ones
Section rhythm: number or label sections with small gold kickers; use a decorative but consistent border treatment (e.g., double hairline in gold) rather than mixing many frame styles
features: Click-to-call tel: links on all phone numbers (priest/committee) — the single most used feature for a village temple audience on mobile
WhatsApp deep link (wa.me/91XXXXXXXXXX) with a pre-filled message ('Namaskaram, I want to know about sevas at...') — doubles as booking channel with no backend

UPI QR code image + UPI ID text for donations (static QR generated from the temple/committee bank account; label whose name the account is in) — replaces payment gateway entirely for a no-backend site
Google Maps embed (iframe, no API key needed) plus a 'Get Directions' link; include distance/route from the nearest town and landmarks — critical for a village location
Announcement strip: a single editable line in HTML for festival dates/timing changes; TTD and Yadadri both lead with a flash-news ticker, but a static styled strip works without JS

Festival calendar as a simple static list/table of the year's major festivals with Telugu month names (e.g., Vaisakha Suddha Chaturdasi — Narasimha Jayanti); full panchangam is overkill and goes stale without a backend
Timings presented as a ritual schedule table (Suprabhatam, Abhishekam, Darshanam, Maha Nivedana, close) in the Yadadri style — devotees screenshot this
Photo gallery: 8-15 curated images with a lightweight CSS/JS lightbox; lazy-load them

Bilingual handling: for a static site, mixed labels (English heading + Telugu subline, e.g. 'Darshan Timings / దర్శన సమయాలు') is more maintainable than a full EN/TE toggle; if toggling, do it with data-attributes and a small script, defaulting to the mix
Seva list with prices and 'Book by phone/WhatsApp' CTA instead of an online booking system

Skip for a small temple: live darshan streaming, login/accounts, online hundi with receipts, room booking engines, e-newsletter — these are TTD/ISKCON-scale features that break trust when half-working

SEO/practical basics: Open Graph image (deity photo) for WhatsApp link previews — temple links spread via WhatsApp forwards, so the preview card matters disproportionately; mobile-first layout since traffic will be ~90% phones
pitfalls: Donation section with only a QR image and no account-holder name, bank name, or committee identity — looks like a scam; always print who receives the money

Claiming 80G tax exemption without holding a certificate — small village temples usually don't have one; either state the registration/80G number with PAN, or say nothing about tax benefit (Ram Mandir trust sets the standard: trust name + PAN + exact 80G clause)
Autoplaying audio (bhajans/suprabhatam) — instantly dated and drives mobile users away; offer a play button instead
Broken or placeholder features: 'Live Darshan' links that don't stream, empty gallery categories, 'Online booking coming soon' — a smaller site that fully works beats a big one that half-works
Marquee/blinking text, visitor counters, 'best viewed in 1024x768', last-updated-2019 footers — the classic dated-government-site tells seen on many devasthanam sites
Low-resolution stretched deity photos, watermarked images pulled from Google, or clip-art gods instead of real photographs of THIS temple's moolavirat and utsava murti
Color overload: saffron + crimson + yellow + green + blue in one viewport; premium temple design keeps 3 colors (maroon, gold, ivory) plus photography
Walls of centered italic text for history; break sthala puranam into short paragraphs with a pull-quote sloka
No phone number above the fold, or numbers as images (not tappable tel: links)
Stale time-sensitive content: last year's festival dates, outdated timings — for a no-backend site, keep dated content minimal and generic ('celebrated in Vaisakha masam, May') so it doesn't rot
Rendering Telugu as images or with fonts lacking Telugu glyphs (tofu boxes); always load a proper Telugu webfont and test conjunct characters
English-only content for a village audience, or machine-translated Telugu with wrong honorifics — get the Telugu strings verified by a native speaker

reference_sites.notable: The canonical structure: flash-news ticker, ritual-by-ritual day schedule, named trusts for each donation purpose (SV Annaprasadam, SRIVANI, Pranadana) with explicit '80(G) of Income Tax Act, 1961' notes — the gold standard for donation trust signals, but visually a dense government portal, not a design model

reference_sites.site: yadagiriguttatemple.telangana.gov.in (Yadadri — same deity, Lakshmi Narasimha Swamy)
reference_sites.notable: Most relevant reference: saffron/white palette, gopuram + hill-silhouette logo, bilingual Telugu/English, seva news ticker, seva card grid (E-Hundi, E-Donation, Annadanam, Kalyanotsavam ~₹500 vratham pricing), purpose-labelled phone numbers (reception/accommodation/donation cell), Endowments Department attribution as its main trust signal; also the source for the authentic ritual-timing table format (Suprabhatam 4:00-4:30, Nijabhishekam 5:30-6:30, etc.)

The premium modern benchmark: card-based festival/event grid, clean sans typography with gold accents, restrained palette, prominent Donate button in header, named seva categories (Annadana Seva, Mandira Nirmana), dedicated donor-care email, WhatsApp channel, high-quality daily darshan photography — proof that a temple site can look contemporary without losing sanctity

Site blocks fetchers (ECONNRESET), but secondary sources confirm the standard devasthanam feature set: seva price list (Suprabhata Seva ₹100), e-hundi, downhill/uphill accommodation info, darshan-block timings — useful as content-structure reference for Narasimha temple sevas like Chandanotsavam
Returned 403 to fetchers; known devasthanam pattern of live darshan, e-hundi and accommodation modules — an example of the feature-heavy tier a village temple should NOT imitate

Best-practice donation transparency template: single authorized platform statement, UPI ID printed in text (shriramjanmbhoomi@sbi) alongside QR, trust name + PAN (AAZTS6197B), exact 80G clause cited (80G(2)(b), 50% deduction, cash over ₹2000 ineligible), receipt-issuance process explained — the exact fields a village temple donation block should emulate at smaller scale
